shortDescription.text: IDOR allows any authenticated user to access other users' audit logs
SQL injection via unsanitized user input in query string
Raw exception details leaked to the user in error responses

message.text: The /logs endpoint accepts a user_id query parameter and uses it directly to fetch audit logs without verifying that the authenticated user is authorized to view that user's logs. Any authenticated user can enumerate and read audit logs belonging to any other user by changing the user_id parameter value.

In AuditLogger.get_user_logs(), the user_id parameter is interpolated directly into a SQL query using an f-string: f"SELECT * FROM audit_logs WHERE user_id = {user_id} ORDER BY timestamp DESC". Since user_id originates from the query string (request.args.get('user_id')), an attacker can inject arbitrary SQL. For example, user_id=1 OR 1=1 would dump all audit logs, and UNION-based payloads could extract data from other tables.

In AuditLogger.log_action(), all parameters (user_id, action, details, ip_address) are interpolated into an INSERT SQL statement via f-string formatting. An attacker who can influence any of these values (e.g., through a crafted username during login that flows into details, or via IP address spoofing) can inject arbitrary SQL into the audit_logs table or exfiltrate data.

The /logs endpoint catches exceptions and returns the raw error message to the user via f"Error loading logs: {e}". This can leak internal database structure, table names, and SQL syntax details to an attacker, aiding further exploitation of the SQL injection vulnerability.
